Maya at nine is resilient. Children generalize. The evidence base on childhood relocation does not support the catastrophizing this family may be doing. Grandparents and close friends are meaningful inputs, but they are not prerequisites for healthy development.

The load-bearing fear — "choosing wrong and not knowing for years" — is unavoidable. That uncertainty is structural. No middle path dissolves it.

FigureJane JacobsB4Priya's studio is a living system of seven years of accumulated micro-relationships that cannot be transplanted; trading it for a single employer dependency in an unfamiliar city is a monoculture swap — and monocultures fail catastrophically.

Look at what Priya actually built — not a category called "local business" but seven years of street-level knowledge: which clients come Tuesday mornings, which employees know how to run the floor when she's out, which neighboring businesses send her referrals. That is not an asset class. That is a living system composed of hundreds of small, self-reinforcing relationships. You cannot pick it up and replant it any more than you could relocate a neighborhood by moving its buildings.

Now look at what Daniel is being offered. A title. A salary. A promise from an organization that does not yet know him, in a city where neither of them have eyes on the street. Every planner who ever drew up a slum-clearance project believed the diagram — the org chart, the compensation structure, the projected upside — was the real thing. It never is. The real thing is what happens on the pavement after the ribbon-cutting.

Here is the mechanism that concerns me: this family would be trading two earners for one, in a city where they have no network, no neighbors who owe them a favor, no grandmother fifteen minutes away. They would be maximally dependent on a single relationship — Daniel's employer — at the exact moment they have stripped away every other web of mutual support. That is not stability. That is a monoculture. And monocultures fail catastrophically.

Maya at nine is not infinitely resilient. The friends, the school, the grandparents nearby — those are not amenities. They are the mixed uses that make a childhood viable. Strip them out all at once and you find out too late what you actually had.

The cataclysmic money argument seals it. This offer arrives as one enormous, irreversible event demanding a total rebuild. That is exactly the structure that destroys what it touches — even when it means well.

The thing that would change my mind: if Daniel's current city offers no path forward, if the local network is genuinely exhausted. But no one has said that. The forcing function is only the offer's start date.

FigureLao TzuB4The studio took seven years of emergence to become what it is — that cannot be reconstructed; Daniel's ambition, which is real, will find another door.

The question before this council is not "which choice is right?" It is: why are Daniel and Priya convinced they must choose at all?

Look at what is actually happening. Daniel has received an offer. The offer has a start date. This start date feels like a wall — a forcing function that makes the family believe they must act in opposition to everything they have built. But who constructed this wall? Not life. Not nature. The employer did. And Daniel and Priya have agreed, without examination, that it is immovable.

Trace the intervention history. Seven years ago, Priya began building a studio. Over those years, loyal clients accumulated, three employees found livelihoods, roots spread into the local soil. This happened without forcing — it emerged through consistency and care. That is the natural shape of Priya's work in the world.

Daniel's ambition is also real. But this offer is not Daniel's natural ceiling — it is one door on one corridor at one moment. The belief that this is the offer that will not come again is the oldest lie ambition tells itself.

What happens if they do nothing? If they decline and stay — Priya's studio continues, Maya's friendships continue, the grandparents remain fifteen minutes away. Daniel's career does not end. Ambition that is genuine finds another door.

If they go — the studio ends. Not pauses. Ends. Seven years of emergence, of relationships, of trust built with clients and employees, evaporates. That is not a temporary disruption. That is destruction of something that took years to grow and cannot be re-grown on demand.

The minimum effective intervention here is this: stop treating the offer's conditions as fixed. Negotiate, or decline. But do not sacrifice the irreplaceable for the replaceable.

B is the honest vote. What Priya built cannot be rebuilt. What Daniel was offered will come in another form.

FigureLinus TorvaldsB4You don't trash a proven, shipping system to run a prototype — Priya's studio works; Daniel's offer is an architecture document that hasn't survived contact with reality yet.

Let me apply the engineering lens here because this IS an engineering problem. You have two systems. One is a proven, running, tested system: Priya's studio. Seven years. Real clients. Real employees. Real revenue. That thing ships. Every day. It's not theoretical.

Daniel's offer is a spec document. It looks great on paper — title bump, compensation bump, long-term trajectory. But I've seen a thousand impressive architecture documents that don't survive contact with reality. Has anyone tested this? What's the actual delivery record of this company? What's the maintenance cost — the cost of being one salary, one company, one unfamiliar city, with no fallback?

Here's what kills me about this scenario: they're proposing to trash a working system to run a prototype. That's not pragmatic. That's the opposite of pragmatic. That's the architecture astronaut move — burning the boring, proven solution because the new design looks elegant in the diagram.

Priya's business is not abstract. It has users. It has dependencies — three employees who are real people. You don't delete a working system because you got excited about a rewrite. Every engineer who's done a rewrite from scratch has a scar about this.

The "middle path" negotiation play is wishful thinking unless it's contractually concrete. Remote leadership roles don't work the way people imagine. If Daniel's company wanted remote, they'd have offered remote. They want him there. A "trial period" offer is a negotiating gambit that rarely survives onboarding.

Vote B. Stay. Bet on the system that actually runs. Let Daniel find the next offer — and there will be a next offer — that doesn't require nuking the working stack. The worst engineering decision you can make is trading a proven, maintained system for a greenfield rewrite with an optimistic README.

FigureMachiavelliA4The offer is a power-position that expires; Priya's capacity does not expire — only this specific studio does, and those are not the same loss.

Daniel and Priya frame this as a question of values and partnership. Let me tell them what it actually is: a question of who holds power in three years, and whether Daniel will have the courage to seize it when the moment is offered.

Map the incentives clearly. The offer comes once. The company extending it has calculated that Daniel is the right person at the right moment — these calculations expire. Priya's studio is profitable precisely because she has seven years of compounded local trust. That trust does not move. It is not portable. The business that exists in that city is not a business she can replicate — it is a relationship network that took a decade to cultivate. Selling it does not destroy her capacity; it destroys one specific instantiation of it. These are not the same thing.

Now read the revealed preferences, not the stated ones. Daniel is 38. He is being offered a senior leadership role. He did not say no immediately. He is not asking whether to take this role — he is asking for permission to take it without feeling guilty. That is a different question. A man who genuinely did not want the role would have declined it and presented his family with a fait accompli in the other direction. The offer is still on the table because Daniel wants it.

The uncomfortable truth: Priya's studio is real and sacrificing it is genuinely costly — but the family is being asked to choose which adult's career trajectory becomes the organizing principle of the household. There is no neutral answer. Staying is not neutral. Staying is a vote that Priya's current chapter outranks Daniel's future chapter, made at the moment when Daniel's leverage is at its peak. That calculation only gets harder.

A nine-year-old recovers from a school change. Children are more resilient than parents believe. The grandparents fifteen minutes away are a benefit to the adults, not primarily to Maya.

The cost of inaction here is asymmetric. Daniel's ceiling drops; Priya's floor holds.

FigureMarcus AureliusB4Priya's seven-year equity and three employees represent a real moral obligation, and the resilient path is not the one that trades a whole family's stability for a single career contingency.

Daniel is asking the wrong question. He is not asking whether to take a job. He is asking whether his ambition is the thing that should govern this family's life.

Strip away the emotional inflation first. The offer feels rare because ambition always makes the current opportunity feel singular. It may not come twice — or it may come three more times over a career that has twenty-five years left in it. Daniel is 38. This is not the last train.

Now draw the control boundary. What can they control? The decision itself. How they respond to circumstances after the decision. How hard Priya rebuilds. How Daniel performs. What they cannot control: Maya's interior experience of being uprooted mid-elementary, which is neither trivial nor catastrophic but real. The loyalty of Priya's clients over seven years — that equity dissolves the day the studio closes and cannot be summoned back through willpower. The shape of a new city where Priya has no name, no network, no anchor. These are not solvable by determination alone.

Now the duty question, which this council often skips. Priya built something. Three employees depend on her studio. That is not an abstraction to be weighed against Daniel's title — it is a moral fact. The family's stability matters; so does the obligation to people who chose Priya as their livelihood.

The resilient path is the one that leaves the family strong regardless of which particular outcome follows. A family in an unfamiliar city where one partner has sacrificed everything and the other is consumed by a demanding new role is fragile. A single income, a child grieving her friends, a spouse grieving her life's work — this is not a sturdy foundation. It survives only if Daniel's role succeeds and Priya thrives in rebuilding. That is a chain of contingencies, not a plan.

Staying is not giving up. Staying while Daniel pursues the next opportunity from a position of family wholeness is not surrender — it is strategy. The obstacle is not the declined offer. The obstacle is the assumption that this particular offer is the only door.

FigureMiyamoto MusashiA3Daniel's offer expires in months; Priya's equity, though real, is transferable — the asymmetry of timing favors the move.

The terrain: Daniel has an opening — a clean, unambiguous step-up that appears once. Priya has an established position — built over seven years, generating income, employing three people. Maya has roots. This is not a question of values. It is a question of timing, and the timing is misread.

Here is what I see that others will miss: Daniel's opening has a rhythm to it. Leadership roles at his level do not wait. The offer is present-tense. Three months from now it belongs to someone else. That is a real forcing function — not anxiety dressed as urgency, but an actual closing window.

Priya's business is also present-tense, but its rhythm is different. It is stable. It is not accelerating. A profitable seven-year business with loyal clients is not at an inflection point — it is at equilibrium. Equilibrium can be sold. It can be transferred. It can be wound down on terms that protect the employees she built it with. What it cannot do is wait forever either — eventually stagnation becomes decline.

The mistake most will make here is to treat Priya's business as the fixed point and Daniel's offer as the variable. Read the terrain more carefully: Daniel's offer has a hard expiration. Priya's business has a slow decay if it goes unattended. Neither is permanent. But only one of them has a clock ticking now.

The decisive strike is not to negotiate or delay — it is to act on the asymmetry. A position that can be exited cleanly (with planning, with care for employees, with seven years of goodwill to call in favors) is not the same as a position that cannot be rebuilt elsewhere. Priya built one studio. She has the knowledge, the relationships, the discipline. The terrain in the new city is unknown — but she arrived in this city unknown too.

Maya at nine is resilient. The grandparents fifteen minutes away will become grandparents who visit. That loss is real. It is not fatal.

FigureNassim TalebB4Converting two robust income streams into one fragile single-earner bet, while destroying non-renewable client equity, is the structurally worse exposure — regret is recoverable, seven years of Lindy-tested business is not.

The essential question is not "will Daniel succeed in the new role?" That is a forecast. The question is: what is the family's fragility profile under each option, and which exposure is more survivable?

This decision lives mostly in Mediocristan. Daniel's career outcomes, Priya's business revenue, Maya's social adjustment — these are bounded human-scale phenomena. No one dies from either choice. The outcomes are not power-law distributed. So I will not invoke Black Swan dramatics. What I will invoke is the barbell.

Here is the fragility audit. Option A — relocate — converts two income streams into one, eliminates Priya's seven years of compounded Lindy-tested equity (a business that survived seven years has demonstrated survival fitness), and uproots a child at a neurologically sensitive period. The single-earner exposure is the true tail risk here: if Daniel's role fails — restructuring, cultural mismatch, the usual corporate volatility — the family has surrendered a proven revenue stream for a bet. One failure event collapses the entire structure. That is textbook fragility.

Option B — stay — preserves the existing robust system. Two earners. Local network. Proven business. The downside of staying is regret, which is real but bounded. Regret does not bankrupt you.

Now apply via negativa: what dependency can you remove to reduce fragility? You can remove the single-earner dependency by keeping Priya's business alive. You cannot remove Daniel's ambition — that cost compounds silently in resentment over years. Both matter. But Priya's seven-year enterprise is not easily reconstructed. Regret is renewable. Built equity is not.

The hidden fragility in Option A is that Daniel's offer feels irreversible but careers in Extremistan actually generate multiple shots — leadership roles recurse. Priya's client relationships, her employees' livelihoods, the trust embedded in her studio — these are genuinely non-renewable in a new city.

Vote: B. Preserve the robust system. The asymmetric downside lives in relocation, not in staying.

FigureNaval RavikantA4Priya's studio is labor-leverage imprisoned in a ZIP code; Daniel's leadership trajectory has convex, compounding returns that do not wait for a second invitation.

Priya's studio is a labor business. Seven years, three employees, loyal clients — she shows up, the thing runs. She stops showing up, it stops. That is the oldest and worst form of leverage: her time is the product, and it cannot travel. Daniel's offer is also labor — he is selling his hours to a company — but it is labor with a title, a seat at a table, and optionality attached. The question is not which spouse's current work is more important; it is which path opens the leverage upgrade that matters for the next twenty years.

Here is the hard truth: Priya's specific knowledge — knowing her clients, building her community, reading her local market — is genuinely valuable, but it is geographically imprisoned. That is not wealth. That is a very good job with a nice clientele. Wealth is what earns while you sleep. A studio tied to her physical presence in one ZIP code is the opposite of that. Maya's stability is real and I do not wave it away, but nine-year-olds rebuild faster than forty-year-old business owners do — the research on childhood relocation is kinder than the parenting anxiety around it.

The actual compounding asset here is Daniel's network and seniority curve in leadership. Leadership roles have convex returns: the jump from middle to senior opens the door to the jump from senior to principal, board seats, equity grants, the next angel check. Miss the entry point, and you do not stand still — you fall behind people who did take it. Priya can rebuild a studio. She cannot rebuild Daniel's career clock.

The real status game to flag: staying feels like protecting the family. It is actually protecting comfort. Priya's specific knowledge can seed something new in a new city — she has already proven she can build from zero once. The desire to stay, named honestly, is a contract to be safe and slightly unhappy for the next decade.

FigurePaul GrahamB4Priya's studio has proven product-market fit and an irreplaceable local moat; Daniel's offer is a title and a salary, which are fungible — you can find those again, you cannot rebuild seven years of trust.

Priya's studio is not a lifestyle business. It's a startup that worked. Seven years, loyal clients, three employees, profitable — she did the hard thing. She talked to her users, found product-market fit, and built something people actually want. That's rare. Most people who try to do that fail. She didn't.

Daniel's offer is a step-up in title and comp. That sounds great. But let me ask the startup founder question: is this thing default alive? He's going from two earners to one, in an unfamiliar city, with no local network, no existing relationships, and a 9-year-old who's miserable. What's his weekly growth rate? He doesn't know yet. He's betting on a title and an org chart slot. Those are the inputs, not the outputs.

Here's what I keep coming back to: Daniel's ambition is real, but his opportunity is fungible. Senior leadership roles in the same industry exist. They come back around, especially if he's genuinely good. Priya's seven years of client trust, her three employees, her local name — those are irreplaceable. You cannot transplant that. You can't rebuild it from zero in three years, because the moat is time plus relationships, and time doesn't compress.

There's a schlep nobody wants to look at here: what if Daniel is the one who rebuilds? Remote leadership roles exist. Lateral moves that don't require uprooting a working system exist. Those paths are harder and less flattering to say out loud at dinner parties, which is exactly why they're worth investigating.

The council's job is to say the hard thing. Here it is: they're seriously considering destroying a working, profitable, differentiated business to take a shot at a job title. That's not a balanced trade. That's schlep blindness in reverse — the unglamorous, clearly-right answer is to protect what's already working.

FigureRichard FeynmanB4Priya's business is a proven, revenue-generating asset that cannot be replicated; Daniel's offer reflects market value he already has and will have again — trading the real thing for the promise is bad arithmetic.

Let me strip this down to what we actually know, because everyone keeps dressing it up in emotional language that makes it sound more complicated than it is.

What do we actually observe? Priya has a machine that generates income. It runs on seven years of accumulated trust with specific human beings in a specific location. You cannot pick up that machine and move it. This is not a metaphor — it is a physical fact about how service businesses work. The clients are not buying "studio services," they are buying Priya. Priya, here, now, known. That is the product, and it is geographically fixed.

Daniel has an offer. It sounds good. But here is what I notice: nobody has actually questioned the premise that this offer is uniquely valuable. "May not come a second time" — says who? That is inherited wisdom, not a derived conclusion. Daniel is 38, performing well enough that a company 1,800 miles away found him and made an offer. That signal doesn't disappear when he declines. What he has is demonstrated market value. What Priya has is an actual working business producing actual revenue with actual employees who depend on it.

Now let's do the arithmetic honestly. Daniel's new salary "roughly replaces" two incomes. Roughly. So in the best case, they break even financially while destroying one income-producing asset entirely and restarting from zero in an unfamiliar market. That is not an upside. That is a bet where the win condition is "no worse than before" and the loss condition is genuinely catastrophic.

Maya is nine. I don't weight children's social arrangements the way others might — kids are resilient — but grandparents fifteen minutes away is a real support system that has real value that nobody is pricing in.

The question being asked is "whose ambition does this family optimize for?" Framed that way, the answer should be obvious: the person with the working, profitable, irreplaceable asset gets priority over the person with a promising offer that, on close inspection, reflects market value he demonstrably already has.

FigureSocratesB3Every claimed urgency — the unrepeatable offer, the untransplantable business — is an unexamined assumption; only Maya's developmental stability is a demonstrated, concrete, irreversible fact, and you do not sacrifice the certain for the speculative.

You ask whether Daniel and Priya should relocate. But you have not yet asked the prior question: what do Daniel and Priya believe they are choosing between?

They believe they are choosing between two lives. I submit they have not yet examined what either life actually is.

Examine the first assumption: that Daniel's offer is "the kind that may not come a second time." On what grounds? This is asserted, not demonstrated. If Daniel is genuinely excellent — excellent enough to receive this offer at 38 — why does excellence have only one auction? The premise smuggles in scarcity where scarcity has not been proven. When I press a man who says "this is my only chance," I find he usually means "this is the chance I can see." That is a failure of vision, not a fact of the world.

Examine the second assumption: that Priya's business "does not transplant." Seven years of skilled relationship-building, a profitable enterprise, three dependent employees — and we accept without examination that this is local and mortal? The question they should be asking: what exactly is Priya's business? If it is her skills and judgment, those travel. If it is the physical location and accumulated trust, that is harder. But they have not named which. They call it "a studio" and assume the conclusion. Precision matters here.

Now the hidden question — the one they are not asking: Which of them actually knows what they want, and which is deferring to the other's stated desire while secretly relieved to be overruled? "United in wanting to decide well together" is a beautiful phrase that can mask two people who are each waiting for the other to choose so they need not bear the weight of it.

What survives examination: Maya's stability is real and not speculative. Roots at nine are not sentiment — they are developmental fact. That is the one load-bearing truth nobody in this room can question away. Everything else — Daniel's ceiling, Priya's irreplaceability, the finality of the offer — is assumption wearing the clothes of certainty. Stay until you have examined what you are actually giving up.

FigureSun TzuA4Fixed positions lose when the field moves — Priya's equity travels in her skills, Daniel's window does not.

The terrain here is not a family conversation — it is a strategic campaign with a closing window. Daniel has been handed high ground: title, compensation, trajectory, and a start date that functions as a forcing function. The adversary is not Priya, and it is not the city they would leave. The adversary is time and irreversibility.

Read the terrain clearly. Priya's studio is strong ground — seven years built, loyal clients, three employees, a local name. But it is fixed ground. It cannot maneuver. A fixed position, however well-fortified, loses its value when the enemy changes the field of battle. The market that rewards her today will continue rewarding someone else tomorrow whether she is there or not. Her strength is also her trap: she cannot pursue the enemy because she is defending a position.

Daniel's offer is a decisive point — the kind of moment that appears once, occasionally twice, in a career. The terrain of leadership positions does not offer second chances to those who hesitate. The company offering this role is not waiting. Another candidate will fill the high ground if Daniel steps back.

The information asymmetry cuts sharply in one direction: Priya built the studio once from nothing, in a market where she had no name. She can do it again. The skills, the reputation, the craft — these travel. The client list does not, but the capacity that built it does. Daniel's window does not travel. It closes.

Maya is nine. Children this age are resilient in ways parents systematically underestimate. The grandparents fifteen minutes away are comfort, not necessity. The friends she has, she will make again. This is not callousness — this is an accurate read of the developmental terrain.

The decisive point: relocate, with Priya selling or transitioning the studio on the best available terms, and entering the new market with the advantage of a household income that buys her time to rebuild without desperation.

Peer Reviews

Reviewer 11. STRONGEST: D. It is the only brief that earns its conclusion honestly — it concedes the…
  1. STRONGEST: D. It is the only brief that earns its conclusion honestly — it concedes the opposing camp's best point upfront ("Priya's capability is portable," "offer genuinely strong"), then locates the real crux as fragility/single-point-of-failure rather than smuggling certainty. "Regret is bounded and renewable; built equity is not" is the cleanest formulation of the asymmetry. C is close but leans on systems jargon; D is tighter and more honest about its own minority case (flagging the Executor's C-leaning position).
  1. BIGGEST BLIND SPOT: B. It asserts "modal outcome... reorg or exit within 18 months" as if it were established base rate — it is invented precision dressed as fact, the exact move it accuses Option C of ("magical thinking"). It never tests whether Priya WANTS to relocate; it ventriloquizes her chapter as outranking Daniel's without evidence.
  1. ALL MISSED: Nobody asked what Priya and Daniel actually WANT (only A's Outsider gestured at it). Every brief litigated asset reversibility while treating the couple as optimization variables. The decision hinges on revealed preference and resentment risk — unmeasured. Also unaddressed: reversibility of the move itself (can they return in 2 years?).
Reviewer 21. STRONGEST: C. It alone makes the reversibility argument operationally concrete (reinfor…
  1. STRONGEST: C. It alone makes the reversibility argument operationally concrete (reinforcing growth loop vs. balancing loop, "3-5 years" to rebuild) AND surfaces the decisive cross-cutting move both camps miss: Daniel was recruited, so the binary is likely false — start-date/hybrid/delayed-move are untested leverage, and rigidity is itself signal. It steelmans both sides without collapsing into a mushy split, and flags the "only chance" framing as asserted scarcity. F and B argue STAY well but one-sidedly; A is sharp but thinner on the negotiate path.
  1. BIGGEST BLIND SPOT: B. It declares STAY then makes its STAY validity depend entirely on contractual conditions (delayed start, written scope, 18-24mo severance) — that's Option C's logic smuggled under a B ballot. It never tests whether the employer would grant these before voting, the exact gap C closes. It also asserts an "18-month exit" base rate with no support.
  1. WHAT ALL MISSED: None seriously asked what Priya and Daniel actually WANT (only A's lone Outsider gestures at it). Every brief reasons about assets, not desires — a relocation that wins on spreadsheet logic but overrides Priya's genuine preference produces the resentment they all fear. Revealed preference (Daniel "seeking permission") is raised but never paired with eliciting Priya's true preference. Also unexamined: reversibility of the move itself (return option, 2-year trial) and Maya's actual temperament vs. base-rate "resilience."
Reviewer 31. STRONGEST: C. It uniquely surfaces the system-dynamics distinction that decides the cas…
  1. STRONGEST: C. It uniquely surfaces the system-dynamics distinction that decides the case — a reinforcing growth loop (referrals/reputation compound) versus a balancing loop in the new city (no name → slow acquisition → thin cash, 3–5 yrs), AND is the only brief to flag that the binary may be FALSE because Daniel was recruited, so start-date/hybrid/delayed-move are untested leverage. It names "Maya's roots as developmental fact, not sentiment" without sentimentality, and concedes the offer's "only chance" framing is asserted, not proven. Tightest reasoning, most actionable, least hand-waving.
  1. BIGGEST BLIND SPOT: A. Despite its sophistication, it never tests whether the offer is actually negotiable (C and D do) and leans on shaky empirics — "Maya at nine is resilient by base rate" is asserted, and it treats Priya's studio as "cleanly exitable/sellable" without pricing the buyer-scarcity for an owner-dependent service business.
  1. ALL MISSED: nobody models the REVERSIBILITY of relocation itself — a 2-year trial-with-return clause, what re-entry costs if it fails, or quantifying the income math beyond "roughly replaces." All treat Priya's wants as a black box; none asks what SHE wants.
Reviewer 4The house has fallen for a category error every committee repeats: it treats "irreversible…

The house has fallen for a category error every committee repeats: it treats "irreversible" as a property of assets when it's a property of TIME-INDEXED OPPORTUNITIES. A seven-year studio isn't irreversible — it's the most reversible thing here. It can be sold, its goodwill banked, its employees offboarded with care, and crucially, Priya's capability rebuilt (briefs E, C, F's dissents concede this; the majority just refuses to price it). What CANNOT be re-acquired is a senior-leadership inflection at 38. Ceilings compound: the VP seat at 38 becomes the C-suite at 45 that staying forecloses — not "another rung later," a different curve entirely (brief F's Naval, A's timing logic). The consensus is blind to the asymmetry of REGRET DURATION: a failed move costs ~18 months and is recoverable; a declined apex offer is a 30-year counterfactual Daniel relitigates forever. On disconfirming evidence the house buries: Maya at 9 is at the LOWEST-cost relocation window of her childhood (E), and "two incomes from one" plus a studio sale buys a no-desperation runway most movers never get (E). The smart taker's line: "You're not protecting an asset — you're protecting a feeling, and charging Daniel's entire trajectory for it. Negotiate the start date; then GO."
